title: Portfolio
description: Built a personal portfolio website to showcase data science projects, experience, and resume in a unified, maintainable site. Implemented the entire frontend in vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript ES modules with no framework or build step, using a single `data/site.js` file as a shared data layer consumed by all pages. Automated GitHub repository metadata syncing via custom Node.js scripts that merge live repo data into the data layer without overwriting manually authored fields like project descriptions and categories.

title: AI-Driven Resume Quality & ATS Analysis System
description: Built an agentic resume tailoring system that ingests a user's resume, GitHub repositories, and LinkedIn profile into a skills knowledge graph, then deploys a LangGraph pipeline with Anthropic/OpenAI LLMs to generate ATS-optimized, job-specific resumes through a React chat interface. The backend, built with FastAPI and SQLModel, links skills to concrete evidence from the user's history rather than keyword-stuffing, and used a best-of-N candidate selection strategy with early exit to maximize ATS scores. Deployed the full-stack application via Docker to Fly.io with Supabase Postgres in production, supporting iterative resume revision through natural language chat commands.

title: AI Agent Policy Optimization
description: Built a reinforcement learning policy optimizer for an AI-driven HVAC contractor follow-up agent, framing the multi-step customer communication workflow as a Markov Decision Process with actions including estimate nudges, objection handling, escalation, and opt-out detection. Implemented a tabular Q-learning approach in pure Python to learn optimal action selection across homeowner states such as open estimates, post-service check-ins, and membership renewals, optimizing a reward function that balanced conversion value against opt-out risk and unnecessary contact costs. The resulting policy demonstrated measurable improvements in simulated conversion rates while reducing premature escalations and customer opt-outs compared to a rule-based baseline.

title: Diginetica: GNN vs. Flat Ranker for Session Recommendation
description: Built an end-to-end relational deep learning pipeline on the Diginetica (CIKM Cup 2016) e-commerce logs, using RelBench to convert the relational database into a heterogeneous graph — 8 node types (items, sessions, users plus view/query/click/queried-item/purchase events) and 22 edge types over 11 foreign-key relations. Engineered an `abs_time` event clock at microsecond resolution by folding intra-session millisecond offsets into the event date, enabling time-aware neighbor sampling that admits only neighbors strictly earlier than a per-row seed time so the receptive field never peeks at the future. Defined two tasks: session-conversion entity classification (binary, ~4% positive rate, AUPRC/AUROC) and next-item link prediction via intra-session leave-last-out on 218K sessions. The GNN is a two-tower heterogeneous GraphSAGE encoder (HeteroEncoder + temporal encoder) splitting into session and item towers scored by dot product, trained with sampled-softmax over in-batch and pool negatives. It is compared against an XGBoost LambdaRank ranker (`rank:ndcg`) over anchor-based 1-hop features — co-view, co-purchase, transition, category, popularity, price, is_repeat. A shared per-row candidate pool (co-view + transition + co-purchase + category + popularity backfill, built on train statistics only) lets both models rank the identical set, isolating ranking quality from candidate recall, with Optuna sweeps (persistent SQLite, median pruning) tuning both on Novel Recall@10. Evaluation decomposes Recall@10, NDCG@10, and MRR across slices — Repeat/Novel, popularity (Head/Torso/Tail), and session length — through a single masked `task.evaluate` code path, alongside structural ablations on hop depth, item-feature capacity, and scoring head (pooled dot vs. maxpool vs. ColBERT-style late interaction).

title: Idx_cont_training
description: Built an end-to-end MLOps pipeline for continuously training machine learning models on real estate listing data, incorporating automated data streaming to keep models updated with fresh market information. The pipeline featured geospatial feature engineering with GeoPandas, automated outlier detection and missingness handling, and gradient boosting models (LightGBM and XGBoost) trained and evaluated using scikit-learn. The system was designed to reduce model drift in dynamic real estate markets by enabling scheduled retraining without manual intervention.

title: Portfolio Live Editor
description: Built an Express.js proxy server that intercepted live HTTP responses from a portfolio site and injected a WYSIWYG editing overlay into every served page. The overlay communicated with a custom REST API to read and write changes directly to the source data file, persisting updates instantly without a build pipeline or recompilation step. This replaced manual file editing with a point-and-click interface layered over the live site, eliminating context-switching during content iteration.

title: Real Time ML Feature Store And Quant Research Platform
description: Built a modular quantitative research platform featuring a real-time ML feature store, event-driven backtesting engine, and tax-loss harvesting rules engine using Python, DuckDB, and Feast. The system ingested daily OHLCV data via yfinance, stored features in a Parquet-backed feature store, and applied wash-sale-aware opportunity identification across lot-level position tracking. A mean-reversion classifier served as a TLH gate, integrating the ML pipeline directly into the backtesting harness for end-to-end strategy evaluation.

title: California Real Estate Price Prediction
description: Built a machine learning web application that predicts single-family residence prices across California using ensemble models including XGBoost and LightGBM, trained on real estate transaction data with features such as location, square footage, and property characteristics. Deployed an interactive Streamlit dashboard that allows users to query property estimates through a searchable interface, with predictions visualized using Plotly for regional price comparisons. Model performance was optimized through scikit-learn pipelines with preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning, yielding actionable price estimates benchmarked against historical market data.

title: eCommerce Session Review Analysis - Predicting Online Shopper Purchase Intent
description: Built classification models using Random Forest and XGBoost on 12,330 e-commerce browsing sessions to predict purchase intent, applying threshold tuning to maximize recall and reduce missed conversions, achieving approximately 90% accuracy. Applied K-Means clustering to segment shoppers into five behavioral archetypes, revealing conversion rates ranging from 0.4% for bounce-prone visitors to 24.3% for high-intent consumers. Identified product browsing depth and administrative page engagement as the strongest purchase predictors using Python, scikit-learn, pandas, and seaborn.

title: Dsc207miniproject
description: Built a data science project using Python to collect, process, and visualize structured datasets through HTTP requests via the Requests library and data manipulation with Pandas and NumPy. Applied statistical analysis and created multi-layered visualizations using Matplotlib and Seaborn to uncover patterns and trends within the data. The project demonstrated proficiency in the full data science pipeline, from raw data ingestion to presentation-ready visual insights within a Jupyter Notebook environment.

title: Sleepercelmanual
description: Built a manual, hands-on implementation of a sleeper cell detection system using Python, pandas, and NumPy to process and evaluate network or behavioral data fetched via HTTP requests. The project applied data manipulation and statistical techniques through Jupyter Notebooks to identify dormant or low-activity patterns consistent with sleeper cell behavior. This version served as a ground-truth reference implementation, enabling validation of automated approaches against manually constructed logic.

title: SleeperCel
description: Built a full-stack AI-powered roster building assistant for fantasy football that integrates with the public Sleeper API to display user leagues, rosters, and available players in a React + Vite frontend. Leveraged Python with pandas and numpy in Jupyter Notebooks to process player and roster data, enabling data-driven roster recommendations. The prototype demonstrates end-to-end data flow from live API ingestion through analytical processing to an interactive browser-based interface.

title: DSC 80 Project Notebook
description: Without access to the actual notebook contents, I can only describe the project based on the detected metadata. This DSC 80 project applied data science techniques using pandas for data manipulation, scikit-learn for machine learning modeling, and Plotly and Matplotlib for data visualization. The project followed a structured data science workflow encompassing data cleaning, exploratory analysis, feature engineering, and predictive modeling on a structured dataset.

title: UCSD Research Lab
description: Conducted research at UC San Diego under the supervision of Andre Gray, applying statistical modeling and machine learning techniques in Python (Jupyter Notebook) and R to investigate domain-specific research questions. Developed and iterated on data pipelines, visualization workflows, and quantitative analyses to support ongoing lab objectives. Contributed reproducible, notebook-based research artifacts that documented methodology and findings for collaborative use within the lab.

title: Bike Traffic in Cambridge Interactive Visualization
description: Built an interactive visualization of bicycle traffic patterns across Cambridge using JavaScript, CSS, and HTML. Implemented dynamic filtering and map-based rendering to display bike lane usage and traffic volume across different times and locations throughout the city. The project enabled users to identify high-traffic cycling corridors and underserved areas within Cambridge's bike infrastructure network.

title: Desktop Tutorial
description: This introductory project demonstrated the foundational GitHub workflow by initializing a repository, editing a README file, and committing changes using GitHub Desktop. The exercise covered core version control concepts including local repository management, file staging, and syncing with a remote repository on GitHub.com. The project established a working development environment for future collaborative and solo coding projects.

title: How Airbnb Tourism was impacted by the San Diego Sunshine
description: A static graph which explores Airbnb bookings and revenue throughout the year in San Diego, California. The graph shows how the number of bookings and revenue changes throughout the year, with the highest peaks in the summer months.

title: Study on the Impact of Estrus on Female Rats Throughout the Day
description: This graph is a static visualization of the body temperature across male and female mice. Used to explore differences in the daily cylce of body temperature while highlighting the impact of female estrus.

title: AllRecipes Rating Predictive Classification Model
description: This machine learning model predicts the ratings of recipes on AllRecipes.com based on features of the recipe. We created a multiclass-classifier using a RandomForest.

title: Study of the Presence of Educational Institutions and Income Throughout California
description: An exploratory data analysis of the presence of educational institutions and income throughout California. The project uses a heatmap to show the distribution of educational institutions and income throughout the state of California.

title: Image Processor
description: Built an image processor that can read in an image, apply a filter to it, and then output the processed image. The image processor can apply filters such as negate, grayscale, and edge detection. This project was built using Python.

title: Asteroid Landfall Simulation
description: Written using pandas in the Jupyter Notebook environment, this project simulates the landfall of asteroids using historical datasets, probabilistic modeling, and bootstrapping.

title: Interactive Visualization of Mice Body Temperature Across Gender
description: An interactive visualization of the body temperature across the gender of mice. The visualization allows users to explore the differences in body temperature while allowing filtering and the altering of scope. Features a tooltip which highlights Mouse ID allowing us to examine indvidual mice along with their estrus cycle( should they be female).
